جفتی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian جُفتی (juftī, “pairing; copulation”). By surface analysis, جفت (juft, “pair, couple”) + ـی (-ī, suffix forming abstract nouns).

senses.g: Jagannath, one of the names of Krishna, an avatar of Vishnu

جگنو.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀚𑁄𑀇𑀁𑀕𑀡 (joiṃgaṇa), from Sanskrit ज्योतिरिङ्गण (jyotiriṅgaṇa). Cognate with Maithili जोगनी (jōgᵊnī).

جگہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian جایگاه (jāygāh), جاگه (jâgah), from جا (jâ), جای (jây, “place”) + ـگاه (-gâh, “location suffix”). Compare Old Deccani جاگاہ (jāgāh). Cognate with Marathi जागा (jāgā), Bengali জায়গা (jaẏga), Assamese জেগা (zega).

ex.u:
جل میں ٹھہرا ترا مرا چہرا
کیسا دریا تھا وہ سبھا کیسی
jal mẽ ṭhahrā tirā mirā cehrā
ex.r: kaisā dariyā thā voh sabhā kaisī
ex.e: (please add an English translation of this quotation)

senses.g: to light; turn on (a light, lamp)
to take advantage of another's misfortune

جلتے گھر میں ہاتھ سینکنا.e: Literally, "to warm one's hand in a burning house". Compare the Kashmiri proverb أکِس دَزان دٲر تہٕ بیٛاکھ وُشناوان اَتھٕ (literally “one's beard is burning and another warms their hand”) with a similar meaning.

senses.g: volume or copy (of a book or magazine)

جلد.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian جلد (jild), from Arabic جِلْد (jild).

ex.u: جلدی کیجیے!
ex.r: jaldī kījīye!
ex.e: make haste, hurry up

جلسہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian جلسه (jalsa), from Arabic جَلْسَة (jalsa).

جلق.e: From Classical Persian جلق (jalq), from Arabic زَلَق (zalaq).

جلنا.e: Urdu verb set جَلْنا (jalnā) جَلانا (jalānā) جَلْوانا (jalvānā) Etymology tree Proto-Indo-European *ǵwelH- Sanskrit ज्वल् (jval) Sanskrit ज्वलति (jvalati) Prakrit 𑀚𑀮𑀇 (jalaï) Urdu جَلْنا Inherited from Middle Hindi جلنا (jlna /⁠jlna⁠/) (c. 1649), from Prakrit 𑀚𑀮𑀇 (jalaï), from Sanskrit ज्वलति (jválati). Cognates Cognate with Konkani जळ्चे (j̈aḷce), Gujarati જળવું (jaḷvũ), Marathi जळणे (j̈aḷṇe), Punjabi ਜਲਣਾ / جَلݨا (jalṇā), Nepali जल्नु (jalnu), Assamese জলা (zola).

جلوس.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian جلوس (julūs), from Arabic جُلُوس (julūs).

senses.g: the ceremony when the bridegroom sees the bride in a mirror
the meeting of the bride and bridegroom (in the presence of their relations)

جلوہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian جِلْوَه (jilwa), borrowed from Arabic جِلْوَة (jilwa).

جلیبی.e: Perhaps from Arabic جُلَاب (julāb), which would make it a doublet of گلاب (gulāb).

ex.u: آٹھوِیں جَماعَت
ex.r: āṭhvī̃ jamā'at
ex.e: 8th class.

جماعت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian جَمَاعَت (jamā'at), borrowed from Arabic جَمَاعَة (jamāʕa).

ex.u: جمعدار کوٹ میں رہتے ہیں یا اپنے گھر میں؟
ex.r: jamadār kōṭ mē̃ rahtē ha͠i yā apnē ghar mē̃?
ex.e: Does the jemadar live in the barracks or in his own house?

جمعدار.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian جمعدار (jam'-dār), from Arabic جَمْع (jamʕ, “gathering”) + Persian ـدار (-dār, “bearer”).

جمعرات.e: First attested in c. 1669 as Middle Hindi جمعرات (jm'rat /⁠juma'rāt⁠/), a contraction of جُمْعَہ رات (jm'h rat), from جُمْعَہ (jum'a, “Friday”) + رات (rāt, “eve”), literally “Friday eve”, because as per Islamic and Abrahamic tradition, the day changes at sunset.

senses.g: a gym, arena (tournament which takes place on a Friday)

جمعہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian جُمْعَه (jum'a), borrowed from Arabic جُمْعَة (jumʕa), from جَمَعَ (jamaʕa).

جملہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian جُمْلَه (jumla), from Arabic جُمْلَة (jumla).
Borrowed from Classical Persian جُمْلَه (jumla), from Arabic جُمْلَة (jumla).
Borrowed from Classical Persian جُمْلَه (jumla), from Arabic جُمْلَة (jumla).

جمنا.e: Urdu verb set جمنا جمانا جموانا (jmvānā) Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit *𑀚𑀫𑁆𑀫𑀤𑀺 (*jammadi), from Sanskrit यम्यते (yamyáte), from the root यम् (yam). Cognate with Gujarati જામવું (jāmvũ), Marathi जमणे (jamṇe), Bengali জমা (joma), Assamese জমা (zoma).

senses.g: Jammu (a city, the winter capital of Jammu and Kashmir, India)
Jammu (a region of Jammu and Kashmir, India)

جموں.e: Inherited from Sanskrit जम्बू (jambū). Traditionally considered to be named after Jambu Lochan. Cognate with Saraiki ڄَمُّوں (ǰammūṉ).

senses.g: alternative form of جموں و کشمیر (jammū̃-o-kaśmīr): Jammu and Kashmir (a union territory in northern India; capital: Jammu and Srinagar)
Jammu and Kashmir (a union territory of India)
Jammu and Kashmir, a princely state which governed the region of Kashmir, the territory of which is now de facto divided between China, India, and Pakistan.

جموں و کشمیر.e: From جَمُّوں (jammū̃, “Jammu”) + و (o, “and/&”) + کَشْمِیر (kaśmīr, “Kashmir”), likely a calque of English Jammu and Kashmir. Compare Hindi जम्मू और कश्मीर (jammū aur kaśmīr).

جمہوری.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian جمهوری (jumhūrī), from Arabic جُمْهُورِيَّة (jumhūriyya).

senses.g: Democratic Republic of the Congo (a country in the Central Africa, Africa)

جمہوری جمہوریہ کانگو.e: From Urdu جمہوری (“democratic”) + Urdu جمہوریہ (“republic”) + Urdu کانگو (“Congo”).

جمہوریت.e: Most likely from Ottoman Turkish جمهوریت (cumhûriyet, “republic”), formed from جمہور (jamhūr, “populace, demos”) + ـیت (-et).

ex.u: جمہوریہ ترکیہ
ex.r: jamhūrīya-e turkīye
ex.e: Republic of Türkiye

senses.g: form of address for a judge

جناب.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian جَنَاب (janāb), from Arabic جَنَاب (janāb).

senses.g: a surname, Jinnah, from Gujarati [in turn originating as a patronymic], held by the Jinnah Family
a surname, Jinnah, from Gujarati [in turn originating as a patronymic], held by the Jinnah Family — Muhammad Ali Jinnah

جناح.e: Borrowed from Arabic جُناح (junāḥ, “blame”). Doublet of گُناہ (gunāh).

ex.u: اٹھے جنازہ جو کل ہمارا قسم ہے تم کو نہ دینا کندھا
ex.r: uṭhe janāzā jo kal hamārā qasam hai tumko nā denā kāndhā
ex.e: If my funeral is tomorrow, promise me you won't help lift my bier

senses.g: bier; the corpse (or coffin in which a corpse is taken away)

جنازہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian جَنَازَه (janāza), borrowed from Arabic جَنَازَة (janāza). First attested in c. 1503 as Middle Hindi جنازہ (janāza).

جنت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian جَنَّت (jannat), borrowed from Arabic جَنَّة (janna). First attested in c. 1611 as Middle Hindi جنت (jnt /⁠janat⁠/).

senses.g: a female given name from Arabic

جنت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian جَنَّت (jannat), borrowed from Arabic جَنَّة (janna). First attested in c. 1611 as Middle Hindi جنت (jnt /⁠janat⁠/).

senses.g: people, public, common folk – synonym of عَوَام ('avām)

ex.u: میرا اور ان کا گوشت پوست ایک ھے، میں جنتا کا بیٹا ھوں، خادم ھوں !
ex.r: Alexander Fadeyev
ex.e: My flesh and skin and their [flesh and skin] is one, I am a son of the people, a servant!

جنتا.e: Borrowed from Sanskrit जनता (janatā), from जन् (jan), from Proto-Indo-European *ǵenh₁-. Compare Spanish gente.

ex.u:
دُکھوں کی گِنْتِی سے کالی ہَے زِنْدَگی کی کِتاب
تو جَنْتَری میں اُنْھیں سال و ماہ لِکھ لِیجے
dukhõ kī gintī se kālī hai zindagī kī kitāb
ex.r: to jantarī mẽ unhẽ sāl o māh likh līje
ex.e:
The book of life is dark from the counting of sorrows
So write them in the calendar by year and month

ex.u: جِنْسی طَور پَر
ex.r: jinsī taur par
ex.e: sexually

جنسی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian جنسی (jinsī), from Arabic جِنْسِيّ (jinsiyy). By surface analysis, جنس (jins) + ـی (-ī).

جنگ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian جَنْگ (jang), from Middle Persian 𐫋𐫗𐫗𐫃 (jnng /⁠ǰang⁠/), from Proto-Indo-European *ǵʰengʰ-.

جنگجو.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian جنگجو (jangjō, literally “war-seeker”).

جنگل.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀚𑀁𑀕𑀮 (jaṃgala), from Sanskrit जङ्गल (jaṅgala, “arid or sterile region”), from a substrate language.

جنگلا.e: Borrowed from Portuguese janela, from Vulgar Latin *januella, from Latin jānua.

جنگی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian جَنْگِی (jangī), from the noun جَنْگ (jang).

ex.u: جَدِید نَثْر اُرْدُو کا جَنَم فورْٹ وِلّیَم کالِج کَلْکَتَّہ میں ہُوا
ex.r: jadīd nasr urdū kā janam forṭ villyam kālij kalkatta mẽ huā
ex.e: The birth of modern Urdu prose occurred in Fort William College, Calcutta

جنوب.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian جَنُوب (janūb), from Arabic جَنُوب (janūb)

جنوبی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian جَنُوبِی (janūbī), Arabic جَنُوبِيّ (janūbiyy).

senses.g: South Africa (a country in the Southern Africa, Africa)

جنوبی افریقہ.e: Borrowed from Arabic جَنُوب أَفْرِيقْيَا (janūb ʔafrīqyā).

senses.g: South Asia (a geographic region of Asia, comprising all the countries of the Indian subcontinent (principally India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, Nepal, and Bhutan) and the Indian Ocean (principally Sri Lanka, Maldives))
South Korea (a country in the East Asia, Asia)

جنوری.e: Borrowed from English January. Possibly through Classical Persian جَنْوَرِی (janwarī).

جنون.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian جنون, from Arabic جُنُون (junūn).

senses.g: janeu (sacred thread worn by Hindu men)

جنیئو.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀚𑀡𑁆𑀡𑁄𑀯𑀯𑀻𑀅 (jaṇṇovavīa), from Sanskrit यज्ञोपवीत (yajñopavītá).

جو.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀚𑁄 (jo), from Sanskrit यः (yáḥ), from Proto-Indo-Aryan *Hyás, from Proto-Indo-Iranian *Hyás, from Proto-Indo-European *Hyós.

جوا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀚𑀽𑀤 (jūda), from Sanskrit द्यूत (dyūta, “gambling”).

جواب.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian جَوَاب (jawāb), from Arabic جَوَاب (jawāb).

senses.g: to refuse; decline; reject (a task; employment; proposal etc.)
to give up (e.g. of a doctor in a patient's treatment in palliative care)

ex.u: اور جو بات نہیں ماننی ہوتی اس کے لیے کوئی جواز پیش کر دیتے ہیں دراصل یہ جواز جھوٹ پر مبنی ہوتا ہے۔
ex.r: aur jo bāt nahī̃ mānnī hotī us ke lie koī javāz peś kar dete ha͠i dar asl ye javāz jhūṭ par mabnī hotā hai.
ex.e: And he gives a justification for whatever he does not wish to accept, but in actual fact that justification is based on lies.

جواز.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian جَوَاز (jawāz), from Arabic جَوَاز (jawāz).

جواز سفر.e: Borrowed from Arabic جواز سفر (jawāz safar).

جوان.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian جَوَان (jawān). Displaced جُْوان (juvān).
Borrowed from Classical Persian جَوَان (jawān). Displaced جُْوان (juvān).

جوت.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀚𑁄𑀢𑁆𑀢 (jotta), from Sanskrit योक्त्र (yóktra, “rope, thong, halter”), from Proto-Indo-Aryan *yáwktram, from Proto-Indo-Iranian *yáwktram, from Proto-Indo-European *yéwg-tro-m, from *yewg- (“to yoke”).

senses.g: a beam or ray of light, the light or flame of a lamp or candle, a halo
a lamp (as placed before an idol)

جوت.e: Semi-learned borrowing from Sanskrit ज्योतिस् (jyotis). Doublet of جیوتی (jyoti), a tatsama.

جوتا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀚𑀼𑀢𑁆𑀢 (jutta, “joined”), from Sanskrit युक्त (yukta, “joined, yoked”) or युक्तक (yuktaka, “pair”).

جوجھ.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀚𑀼𑀚𑁆𑀛 (jujjha), from Sanskrit युद्ध (yuddha, “fight”).

جوجھنا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀚𑀼𑀚𑁆𑀛𑀤𑀺 (jujjhadi), from Sanskrit युध्यते (yúdhyate).

جوڑ.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀚𑁄𑀟 (joḍa), deverbal from 𑀚𑁄𑀟𑁂𑀇 (joḍei), denominal verb from 𑀚𑀼𑀢𑁆𑀢 (jutta), from Sanskrit युक्त (yukta), from Proto-Indo-European *yugtós, from *yewg- + *-tós.

جوڑا.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀚𑁄𑀟 (joḍa) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀓- (-ka-); see there for more.

senses.g: to join, connect, link, unite, attach

جوڑنا.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀚𑁄𑀟𑁂𑀇 (joḍei); see there for more.

جوز.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian جوز (jawz), from Arabic جَوْز (jawz), itself from Middle Persian 𐭢𐭥𐭦 (gōz, “walnut”). Doublet of گوز (goz).

senses.g: a hazardous undertaking, enterprise, or business
valuables (jewels, money, the thing risked or put to hazard, etc.)

جوکھم.e: Inherited from Sanskrit योगक्षेम (yogakṣema, “the security given by possessions, wealth, etc”), from योग (yoga, “junction, work”) + क्षेम (kṣema, “basis, security”). Compare Gujarati જોખમ (jokham), Marathi जोखीम (jokhīm).

senses.g: an itinerant conjurer or snake charmer similar to a gypsy (probably due to the popular association of supernatural or miraculous powers with Sufi monks)

جوگی.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit [Term?], from Sanskrit योगिन् (yogin). Compare Punjabi ਜੋਗੀ (jogī), Bengali যোগী (jōgi), Marathi जोगी (jogī). Equivalent to جوگ (jog, “lit. union [with God], monastic renunciation, asceticism”) + ـی (-ī).

جولائی.e: Borrowed from English July, ultimately from Latin Iulius.

جون.e: Borrowed from English June, ultimately from Latin Iunius.

جوناگڑھ.e: Borrowed from Gujarati જૂનાગઢ (jūnāgaḍh). By surface analysis, جُونا (jūnā, “old”) + گَڑھ (gaṛh, “fortress”).

senses.g: Jaunpur; a district of Uttar Pradesh, India — a city in Jaunpur district, Uttar Pradesh, India
Jaunpur; a district of Uttar Pradesh, India — a Lok Sabha constituency in Uttar Pradesh, India
Jaunpur; a district of Uttar Pradesh, India — a Legislative Assembly constituency in Uttar Pradesh, India

جونپور.e: After Muhammad Fakhr Malik Jauna Khan bin Tughluq; literally Jauna's town, جون (jon) + ـپور (-por).

senses.g: Jaunpuri; of, or relating to Jaunpur.

جوہر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian جَوهَر (jawhar), from Arabic جَوْهَر (jawhar), from Early Classical Persian گَوهَر (gawhar), from Middle Persian gwhl (/⁠gōhr, gōhar⁠/, “substance, essence; jewel”).

senses.g: self-immolation on a pyre, especially that done by the Rajput women when facing capture, as men fought the final battle

ex.u: جَوہَری ہَتِھیار
ex.r: jauharī hathiyār
ex.e: nuclear weapon

جوہی.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀚𑀽𑀳𑀻 (jūhī), from Sanskrit यूथिका (yūthikā)
Inherited from Prakrit 𑀚𑀽𑀳𑀻 (jūhī), from Sanskrit यूथिका (yūthikā)

جہاد.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian جِهَاد (jihād), from Arabic جِهَاد (jihād).

جہاز.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian جَهَاز (jahāz), borrowed from Arabic جَهَاز (jahāz).

senses.g: oblique plural of جَہاز (jahāz, “aircraft; plane; ship”)

جہازی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian جهازی (jahāzī). By surface analysis, جَہاز (jahāz, “ship”) + ـِی (-ī).

ex.u: سِنْدْباد جَہازِی کے حَیرَت اَنْگیز سَفَر
ex.r: sindbād jahāzī ke hairat angez safar
ex.e: the wonderful voyages of Sinbad the sailor

جہازی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian جهازی (jahāzī). By surface analysis, جَہاز (jahāz, “ship”) + ـِی (-ī).

senses.g: Jahangirnagar (former name of Dhaka, capital city of Bangladesh)

ex.u: ڈھاکہ عجیب شہر، نام جہانگیر نگر، دو چار شریف ہے، باقی کٹّی تمام
ex.r: ḍhākā ajīb śahar, nām jahāṅgīr nagar, do cār śarīf hai, bāqī kuṭṭī tamām
ex.e: Dhaka is a strange city, its name is Jahangirnagar, there are a few nobles, the rest are all Kuttis.

جہاں.e: Compare coordinate terms کَہاں (kahā̃, “where”, interrogative), یَہاں (yahā̃, “here”), وَہاں (vahā̃, “there”)

ex.u:
سارے جہاں سے اچھا ہندوستاں ہَمارا
ہم بلبلیں ہیں اس کی، یہ گلستاں ہمارا
sāre jahā̃ se acchā hindostā̃ hamārā
ex.r: ham bulbulẽ ha͠i is kī, ye gulistā̃ hamārā
ex.e:
Better than the whole world, is our Hindustan;
We are its nightingales, and it is our flower-garden.

senses.g: Jhelum (a city in Jhelum district, province of Punjab, Pakistan)
Jhelum (a river in the Punjab of Pakistan and the union territory of Jammu and Kashmir, India)

جہنم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian جَهَنَّم (jahannam). First attested in c. 1564 as Middle Hindi جہنم (jhnm /⁠jahannam⁠/).

senses.g: used to show agreement - yes, correct

ex.u: جی بالکل صحیح!
ex.r: jī bilkul sahīh!
ex.e: Yes, absolutely correct!

senses.g: the honorific particle, usually appended to names (of any gender) and titles

ex.u: صاحب جی
ex.r: sāhib jī
ex.e: Master

جی.e: Inherited from Old Hindi जी (jī), from Prakrit 𑀚𑀻𑀅 (jīa), from Sanskrit जीव (jīva, “long live”). Cognate with Assamese জীউ (ziu).

senses.g: heart, mind (in the figurative, not anatomical, sense)

ex.u: اُس کی محبت نے میرا جی للچایا۔
ex.r: us kī muhabbat ne merā jī lalcāyā
ex.e: My heart was allured by their love.

جی.e: Inherited from Old Hindi जी (jī), from Prakrit 𑀚𑀻𑀅 (jīa), from Sanskrit जीव (jīva, “long live”). Cognate with Assamese জীউ (ziu).

senses.g: a member or supporter of the Pakistan Peoples Party.

جیالا.e: From جی (jī, “heart”) + والا (vālā, “one (who is/has)”). Possibly a borrowing from Punjabi [Term?] or Sindhi [Term?] (where والا (vālā) would be آلا (ālā)).
From جی (jī, “heart”) + والا (vālā, “one (who is/has)”). Possibly a borrowing from Punjabi [Term?] or Sindhi [Term?] (where والا (vālā) would be آلا (ālā)).

جیب.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian جیب (jēb), borrowed from Arabic جَيْب (jayb).

جیبھ.e: From Sanskrit जिह्वा (jihvā), from Proto-Indo-Iranian (compare Avestan 𐬵𐬌𐬰𐬎𐬎𐬁 (hizuuā), Persian زبان (zabân)), from Proto-Indo-European *dn̥ǵʰwéh₂s (compare Irish teanga, Latin lingua, Tocharian A käntu, Tocharian B kantwo, Lithuanian liežùvis, Polish język (“language, people”), Armenian լեզու (lezu), and Old English tunge).

جیت.e: Inherited from Sanskrit जित (jita). Compare Punjabi جِتّ (jitt).

جیٹھ.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀚𑁂𑀝𑁆𑀞 (jeṭṭha, “elder brother”), from Sanskrit ज्येष्ठ (jyeṣṭha, “eldest brother, first, chief”).

senses.g: Jyeshta; the third month of the Nanakshahi and Hindu calendar

جیٹھ.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀚𑁂𑀝𑁆𑀞 (jĕṭṭha), from Sanskrit ज्यैष्ठ (jyaiṣṭha). Cognate with Kachchi જેઠ (jeṭh), Sindhi ڄيٺُ (j̄eṭhu).

جیرا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀚𑀻𑀭𑀕 (jīraga), from Sanskrit जीरक (jī́raka). Doublet of زِیرَہ (zīra). Cognates include Assamese জীৰা (zira), Bengali জিরা (jira), Dhivehi ދިރި (diri), Gujarati જીરું (jīrũ), Marathi जिरीं (jirī̃), Nepali जिरो (jiro), Odia ଜୀର (jira), Punjabi جيرا (jīrā), Sindhi جيِرو (jīro), and Sinhalese දුරු (duru)

senses.g: Jacobabad (a city in Jacobabad district, Sindh, Pakistan)

جیکب آباد.e: From جیکب (“John Jacob (East India Company officer)”) + آباد (ābād, “placename suffix”).

جیل.e: Borrowed from English jail, from Middle English gayole, borrowed from Anglo-Norman jaiole, from Late Latin caveola, from Latin cavea (“cage”) + -ola (diminutive ending).

senses.g: a surname, equivalent to English Jain

جینا.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀚𑀻𑀯𑀇 (jīvaï). Cognate with French vivre and Russian жить (žitʹ).
Inherited from Prakrit 𑀚𑀻𑀯𑀇 (jīvaï). Cognate with French vivre and Russian жить (žitʹ).

جیوتی.e: Learned borrowing from Sanskrit ज्योति (jyoti). Doublet of جوت (jot), a tadbhava.

senses.g: Jiwani (a city in Gwadar district, Balochistan, Pakistan)
victory to Deity Rāma: Jai Shri Ram

جئے شری رام.e: From Sanskrit जय श्री राम (jaya śrī rāma).

جھانجھ.e: Onomatopoeic; from Ashokan Prakrit *𑀛𑀸𑀁𑀛 (*jhāṃjha). Compare Gujarati ઝાંઝ (jhā̃jh), Marathi झांज (jhāñja), Sindhi جهانجهُه (jhā̃jhuh) / झांझु, Odia ଝାଞ୍ଜ (jhāñja), Bengali ঝাঁজ (jhãj)

senses.g: quickly, immediately, swiftly, hurriedly, instantaneously, apace, easily, within no time
shock, jerk, sudden stroke, jolt, shake (often violent and unexpected)

جھٹکا.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀛𑀝𑁆𑀝𑁆 (jhaṭṭ) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀓𑁆𑀓𑁆- (-kk-).

جھکنا.e: Cognate with Nepali झुक्नु (jhuknu), Punjabi ਝੁਕਣਾ (jhukṇā) / جھُکݨا (jhukṇā), Marathi झुकणे (jhukṇe).

جھگڑا.e: From Prakrit *𑀛𑀕𑁆𑀕𑀟 (*jhaggaḍa) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀓- (-ka-), a reconstructed variant of 𑀛𑀕𑀟 (jhagaḍa). See झगड़ना (jhagaṛnā) for more. Cognates Cognate with Punjabi ਝਗੜਾ / جھَگْڑَا (jhagṛā), Sindhi جَهڳڙِو (jhaɠṛo), جهيڙو (jheṛo), Bengali ঝগড়া (jhogoṛa), Assamese জগৰ (zogor).

senses.g: shimmer, glimmer, twinkle (of stars), sparkle (of waves)

ex.u: جَھنْڈے کو لہراؤ!
ex.r: jhanḍe ko lahrā'o!
ex.e: Wave the flag!

جھنڈا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit *𑀥𑀚𑁆𑀚𑀡𑁆𑀟 (*dhajjaṇḍa) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀓- (-ka-), from Sanskrit *ध्वजदण्ड (*dhvajadaṇḍa, “flagstaff”), from ध्वज (dhvaja) + दण्ड (daṇḍa). Cognate with Bhojpuri झंडा (jhaṇḍā), Punjabi ਝੰਡਾ (jhaṇḍā) / جَھنْڈا (jhanḍā), Sindhi جھَنْڊو (jhanḍo) / झंडो (jhaṇḍo), Gujarati ઝંડો (jhaṇḍo), Marathi झेंडा (jheṇḍā), Nepali झण्डा (jhaṇḍā).

جھوٹ.e: From جُھوٹھ (jhūṭh) – a dated form, inherited from Prakrit 𑀛𑀼𑀝𑁆𑀞 (jhuṭṭha).

ex.u: یہ دھوکے باز نے ہَمیں جُھوٹی مَعلُومات دی۔
ex.r: ye dhoke bāz ne hamẽ jhūṭī ma'lūmāt dī.
ex.e: This fraud gave us false information.

ex.u: تھانے دار نے غَدّار سے جُھوٹے دَسْتاویز پَکَڑ لِئے۔
ex.r: thāne dār ne ġaddār se jhūṭe dastāvez pakaṛ lie.
ex.e: The policeman seized counterfeit documents from the traitor.

جھوٹا.e: Inherited from Sanskrit जुष्ट (juṣṭa). Compare Punjabi جُھوٹھا /ਝੂਠਾ (jhūṭhā).

ex.u: تُم جُھوٹے ہو۔
ex.r: tum jhūṭe ho.
ex.e: You are a liar.

جھوٹا.e: Inherited from Sanskrit जुष्ट (juṣṭa). Compare Punjabi جُھوٹھا /ਝੂਠਾ (jhūṭhā).

senses.g: – a sound made prior to attacking.

جھولا.e: Inherited from Sanskrit [Term?], related to *झुल्यति (*jhulyati, “to swing”).

senses.g: Syed Muhammad Usman Marwandi; a Sufi philosopher-poet from Afghanistan buried in modern-day Sindh

ex.u: توں ایں میرا لجپال، جی سیہونی لال
ex.e: You are my preserver-of-honour, oh the red one of Sehwan.

جھولے لال.e: From لال (lāl, “red”) denoting the red attire worn by him.

جھیل.e: Inherited from Prakrit *𑀛𑀺𑀮𑁆𑀮 (*jhilla) (compare 𑀛𑀺𑀮𑁆𑀮𑀇 (jhillaï, “to bathe”), 𑀛𑀺𑀮𑁆𑀮𑀻 (jhillī, “wave”)), of uncertain origin.
Inherited from Prakrit *𑀛𑀺𑀮𑁆𑀮 (*jhilla) (compare 𑀛𑀺𑀮𑁆𑀮𑀇 (jhillaï, “to bathe”), 𑀛𑀺𑀮𑁆𑀮𑀻 (jhillī, “wave”)), of uncertain origin.

senses.g: The eighth letter of the Urdu alphabet, written in the Arabic script; preceded by ج and followed by ح.

چا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian چَا (čā), ultimately from Chinese 茶 (chá).

senses.g: key (object for opening and closing a lock)

چابھی.e: See the etymology of the corresponding lemma form.

چاٹ.e: From چَاٹْنَا (cāṭnā, “to lick”); see there for more.

ex.u: اور چاٹگام میں انکل انور کے ہاں رہوں گا
ex.r: aur cāṭgām mẽ aṅkal anvar ke hā̃ rahūṅgā
ex.e: And uncle Anwar's "yes" will remain in Chittagong.

چاٹگام.e: Possibly from South Asian Classical Persian چاتگام.

چاٹنا.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀘𑀝𑁆𑀝𑁂𑀇 (caṭṭei, “to devour with relish, eat noisily”, onomatopoeia). Compare Gujarati ચાટવું (cāṭvũ), Marathi चाटणे (ċāṭṇe), Bengali চাটা (caṭa).

چادر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian چَادَر (čādar). Doublet of چَھتْری (chatrī).

چار.e: Inherited from Old Hindi चारि (cāri), from Prakrit *𑀘𑀬𑀸𑀭𑀺 (*cayāri), from Sanskrit चत्वारि (catvā́ri), चत्वारः (catvā́raḥ). More information Compare the attested Prakrit 𑀘𑀢𑁆𑀢𑀭𑀺 (cattari). The alteration was probably based off the Sanskrit accusative चतुरः (caturaḥ) without a consonant cluster where intervocalic t > y is expected, but could also be an abnormal numeral phonological development.

senses.g: a bed used in Afghanistan and Pakistan.

چار سو بیس.e: From Section 420 of the Indian Penal Code, which deals with cheating and fraud.

senses.g: a clay ball which kids play with
Charminar (a monument in Hyderabad, Telangana, India to commemorate the shifting of the capital of the Sultanate of Golconda from Golconda to Hyderabad)
Used other than figuratively or idiomatically: see چار (cār), مِینار (mīnār).
Charsadda (a city in Charsadda district, Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, Pakistan)
the middle hour between sunrise and the meridian; forenoon

چاک.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀘𑀓𑁆𑀓 (cakka), from Sanskrit चक्र (cákra, “wheel”), from Proto-Indo-Aryan *ćakrám, from Proto-Indo-Iranian *čakrám, from Proto-Indo-European *kʷékʷlos (“circle, wheel”). Cognate with Marathi चाक (cāk). Doublet of چکر (cakkar, cakra) and چرخہ (carxa).

چال.e: Inherited from Proto-New Indo-Aryan *चल्ला (callā), from Sanskrit *चल्या (*calyā), from चर्या (caryā), from चर् (car, “to move, proceed”) + -या (-yā).

senses.g: challan (for registering a criminal case sent to a court)
list of letters and records sent
a pass; passport; clearance (of a ship)

ex.u: پولس نے چالک سے مال کے بارے سوال کئی
ex.r: polis ne cālak se māl ke bāre savāl kī
ex.e: The police questioned the driver about the goods

senses.g: to turn on, to switch on
the last day of the month.
a loved one (a child or love etc.)

چاند.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi چاند (cand /⁠cā̃d⁠/) (c. 1503), / چند (cnd /⁠cand⁠/) (c. 1435), from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀘𑀁𑀤 (caṃda), from Sanskrit चन्द्र (candrá). Doublet of چَنْدا (candā).

چاندی.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀘𑀁𑀤𑀺𑀆 (caṃdiā), from Sanskrit चन्द्रिका (candrikā, “moonlight; splendour”). By surface analysis, چان٘د (cā̃d/cānd, “moon”) + ـی (-ī).

چاول.e: From Prakrit [script needed] (cāulā), and probably of Dravidian or Austroasiatic origin. Compare Punjabi ਚਾਵਲ (cāval), Punjabi ਚੌਲ (caul), Sindhi چانور (cã̄uru, “grain of rice cleansed from the husk”), Sylheti ꠌꠣꠃꠟ (saul), Bengali চাল (cal), Assamese চাউল (saul), Odia ଚାଉଳ (cāuḷa), Rohingya soil. Related to Persian چلو (čelow), which was borrowed from an Indic language.

چاہنا.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀘𑀸𑀳𑀇 (cāhaï), possibly from Sanskrit काङ्क्षति (kāṅkṣati) or चक्ष॑ते (cákṣate).

senses.g: inflection of چاہْنا (cāhnā, “to will, to desire, ought to”): — masculine plural perfective participle
inflection of چاہْنا (cāhnā, “to will, to desire, ought to”): — masculine plural perfect indicative
inflection of چاہْنا (cāhnā, “to will, to desire, ought to”): — second/third-person singular subjunctive
inflection of چاہْنا (cāhnā, “to will, to desire, ought to”): — third-person singular present/future imperative

ex.u: چاہے برف پڑ رہی ہو تمہیں آنا ہی ہوگا
ex.r: cāhe barf paṛ rahī ho tumhẽ ānā hī hogā
ex.e: You will have to come even if it's snowing.

ex.u: چاہے وہ کراچی چلا جائے چاہے یہاں پر ہی رہے بس ملک چھوڑ کر نا جائے
ex.r: cāhe vo karācī calā jā'e cāhe yahā̃ par hī rahe bas mulk choṛ kar nā jā'e
ex.e: Whether he goes to Karachi or stays here, just let him not leave the country

چای.e: See the etymology of the corresponding lemma form.

چائی.e: See the etymology of the corresponding lemma form.

چپٹی.e: From چپٹ (capaṭ, “stick, adhere; hug, embrace”) + ـی (-ī, “noun forming suffix”).

چپل.e: Borrowed from Telugu చెప్పులు (ceppulu), the plural of చెప్పు (ceppu).

senses.g: Chitkan (a city in Panjgur district, Balochistan, Pakistan)
